ontology.foodex2_description: Cabbage (Brassica oleracea var. capitata), raw, white/green

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of raw shredded white cabbage is approximately 1 cup (90g), commonly used in coleslaw, salads, or as a side vegetable in Singapore economy rice.

nutrient_source.title: White Cabbage (raw) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F14010019; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy (Cabbage, raw, USDA NDB No. 11109)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo Food Code F14010019 is an exact match for raw white cabbage as consumed in Singapore. Values cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy NDB No. 11109 (Cabbage, raw) for consistency. All nutrient values are consistent between both sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database (cabbage, raw)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Raw cabbage GI is reported as approximately 10 (low) in the University of Sydney GI Database and consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 90g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (10 × 5.22) / 100 ≈ 0.5. Carbohydrate per 90g serving = 5.8 × 0.9 = 5.22g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (18mg/100g) and high potassium (170mg/100g) supporting favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Raw white cabbage is an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals due to its very low sodium content (18mg/100g) and meaningful potassium (170mg/100g), which together support a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio; additionally, its glucosinolate-derived isothiocyanates (including sulforaphane) may exert mild cardioprotective and vasodilatory effects, and its vitamin C content (36.6mg/100g) supports vascular endothelial function.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~10), low carbohydrate (5.8g/100g), and dietary fibre (2.5g/100g) attenuating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw white cabbage is highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: its very low GI (~10) and low carbohydrate content (5.8g/100g) result in a negligible glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 0.5 per 90g), while its dietary fibre (2.5g/100g) slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose excursions; folate (43mcg DFE/100g) further supports cellular metabolism.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0g/100g), zero cholesterol, and glucosinolate-derived sulforaphane with anti-inflammatory NF-κB inhibitory activity
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw white cabbage poses no cardiovascular risk and may confer benefit: it contains zero saturated fat and zero cholesterol, while its glucosinolates are metabolised to sulforaphane which inhibits the NF-κB inflammatory signalling pathway; vitamin C (36.6mg/100g) supports collagen synthesis and endothelial integrity, and its very low caloric density (25 kcal/100g) supports weight management relevant to cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sulforaphane (from glucosinolate metabolism) inhibits NF-κB inflammatory pathway; vitamin C supports collagen synthesis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw white cabbage may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as its glucosinolate-derived sulforaphane inhibits NF-κB, a key pro-inflammatory signalling pathway, and its vitamin C content (36.6mg/100g) supports collagen synthesis and joint tissue integrity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Common white cabbage (Brassica oleracea var. capitata) is a pale green, round-headed cruciferous vegetable widely consumed across Singapore households and hawker stalls. It is eaten raw in coleslaw and salads, or cooked in stir-fries, soups, dumplings, popiah (fresh spring rolls), and economy rice dishes. Raw cabbage has a crisp, crunchy texture with a mild, slightly sweet flavour. Nutritionally, it is very low in calories (25 kcal/100g) with a very high water content (~92g/100g), making it an excellent food for weight management. It provides a good source of vitamin C (36.6mg/100g, approximately 40% of Singapore RNI), folate (43mcg DFE/100g), and dietary fibre (2.5g/100g). As a cruciferous vegetable, it contains glucosinolates which are enzymatically converted to bioactive isothiocyanates including sulforaphane upon chewing or cutting; sulforaphane inhibits the NF-κB inflammatory pathway and has been associated with anti-inflammatory and potential cancer-protective properties in epidemiological studies. Its very low GI (~10) and negligible glycemic load make it ideal for diabetic meal planning. Known in Singapore as kobis or ye cai, and in Malay as kubis bulat.

confidence_reason: Exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F14010019) with cross-reference to USDA SR Legacy (NDB No. 11109). All nutrient values are consistent between both approved primary sources. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) are verified and defensible. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature.
notes: saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F14010019 and USDA SR Legacy NDB 11109 — raw white cabbage contains negligible saturated fatty acids. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — plant food contains no dietary cholesterol. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero — plant food contains no vitamin B12. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero — raw cabbage contains no vitamin D. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data not reported in HPB Foodo F14010019; USDA SR Legacy reports trace values below meaningful precision for this food. water_g estimated at 92.2g/100g consistent with USDA SR Legacy NDB 11109 (92.18g). GI of approximately 10 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); glycemic load per 90g serving calculated as (10 × 5.22) / 100 ≈ 0.5. This is a globally cultivated vegetable with origins in Mediterranean Europe; Singapore HPB Foodo is the primary source as this food is commonly consumed in Singapore context.
